speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start with your vitals — your heart rate today is 65 bpm, which is within the normal range.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is 147 mmHg — that’s elevated, so we’ll want to monitor that closely.

speaker: patient
text: Yeah, I’ve been feeling a bit off lately — kind of fatigued and short of breath sometimes.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose came back at 14.2 mmol/L — that’s significantly high, especially for someone not currently in diabetic ketoacidosis.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your labs and imaging, we’re confirming a diagnosis of Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also had some chest tightness over the past few days — mostly when walking up stairs or after meals.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin is low at 97 g/L — consistent with anemia of chronic disease, likely related to your CKD.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Metformin 1000mg twice daily — but given your kidney function, we need to revisit its safety.

speaker: patient
text: Right — my nephrologist mentioned Metformin might not be ideal anymore. What are my options?

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s hold off on further conclusions until we review all your data — including repeat vitals taken just now.

speaker: doctor
text: Actually, your heart rate just measured 89 bpm — up from 65 earlier. Any palpitations or dizziness?

speaker: patient
text: No palpitations, but I did feel lightheaded once yesterday — maybe when standing up quickly.

speaker: doctor
text: Interesting — your systolic blood pressure is now 120 mmHg, down from 147 earlier. That could explain the lightheadedness.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been trying to cut back on salt and drink more water — could that have brought it down?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly — but we also need to consider orthostatic changes and whether your antihypertensives need adjustment.

speaker: patient
text: I haven’t started any new meds — just the Metformin and my usual lisinopril.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your elevated glucose, anemia, and CKD, we’ll need to optimize both glycemic and cardiovascular risk management.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. My last A1c was 9.4%, so clearly things aren’t well controlled.

speaker: patient
text: By the way — that 147 mmHg reading you took first thing? I checked it again at home this morning and got the same number.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your cardiovascular risk profile and that persistent hyperglycemia — which matches the 14.2 mmol/L we saw earlier — I’m going to start you on Atorvastatin 20mg at bedtime to reduce long-term risk.
